concerns.text: The plan defines 'Sparse-Consistency Score' as 're-projection error' (FR-003, SC-001) and uses it as the dependent variable to validate the sparse method's geometric fidelity. However, re-projection error is the direct mathematical output of the RANSAC solver (the predictor). Validating a method's 'fidelity' against its own internal optimization residual is circular. The analysis cannot distinguish between a 'good' geometric model and a 'good' fit to noise without an independent ground truth (e.g., known camera poses or dense depth ground truth) or a distinct metric (e.g., photometric consistency on held-out frames).

The plan proposes a two-way ANOVA with dependent variables 'WorldScore (Dense)' and 'Sparse-Consistency (Sparse)'. These are two different metrics measuring different things (topological fidelity vs. re-projection error). Comparing them directly in an ANOVA to test for 'interaction effects' is a category error. You cannot statistically test if 'Motion x Texture' affects the difference between Metric A and Metric B unless they are on the same scale and measure the same underlying construct. The design conflates 'efficiency' (time) with 'accuracy' (different metrics) without a unified loss function or a clear hypothesis about the trade-off curve.

The plan assumes 'WorldScore' can be computed via a 'pre-trained, frozen geometric consistency model'. In the field of video world models, 'WorldScore' is not a standard, universally defined metric with a public, frozen CPU-compatible implementation. If this metric is defined by the project itself (e.g., based on the same re-projection error or a derived quantity), the validation is tautological. If it relies on an external model not cited or verified, the 'Verified Accuracy' principle is violated. The plan must define the exact mathematical formulation of WorldScore or cite a specific, accessible implementation.

The plan states the dense baseline will be 'generated on-the-fly via a CPU-optimized model (e.g., MiDaS)'. MiDaS is a deep learning model; running it on CPU for 200 video sequences (potentially thousands of frames) to generate dense depth maps for a 'fair comparison' is computationally infeasible within the 6-hour limit and 7GB RAM constraint. The 'apples-to-apples' comparison fails because the sparse method is algorithmic (RANSAC) while the dense baseline is a heavy neural network inference. The time comparison will be dominated by the neural network architecture, not the 'sparse vs. dense' geometric approach, invalidating the efficiency hypothesis.
